title: Quiz: Introduction to Computational Chemistry
description: Test your understanding of computational chemistry methods and their applications

questions.question: Which method is often called the 'gold standard' of quantum chemistry?
questions.explanation: CCSD(T)—Coupled Cluster with Singles, Doubles, and perturbative Triples—is often called the 'gold standard' because it provides chemical accuracy (~1 kcal/mol) for most systems while remaining computationally tractable.

questions.question: What is the main limitation of molecular mechanics (force field) methods?

options.text: They are too slow for large systems
They cannot model bond breaking or forming
They only work for gas-phase molecules

questions.explanation: Molecular mechanics treats atoms as balls connected by springs. It cannot model chemical reactions (bond breaking/forming), electron transfer, or excited states because it doesn't explicitly treat electrons.

questions.question: What does 'ab initio' mean in the context of quantum chemistry?
questions.explanation: Ab initio is Latin for 'from the beginning.' It means the method solves the Schrödinger equation using only fundamental physical constants, without empirical parameters from experiments.

questions.question: Why is DFT (Density Functional Theory) so popular in computational chemistry?

options.text: It is always the most accurate method
It provides a good balance of accuracy and computational cost
It is the easiest to implement

questions.explanation: DFT offers an excellent trade-off: it scales as O(N³), much better than correlated methods like CCSD (O(N⁶)), while providing reasonable accuracy for most chemical problems. It's the workhorse of modern computational chemistry.

questions.question: Full Configuration Interaction (Full CI) gives the exact answer for a given basis set.
questions.explanation: True! Full CI includes all possible electron configurations within a given basis set, giving the exact solution to the electronic Schrödinger equation for that basis. The only error is the basis set incompleteness error.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of quantum computers for chemistry simulations?

options.text: They are faster at all calculations
They naturally represent quantum states, avoiding exponential classical scaling
They can solve the Schrödinger equation analytically

questions.explanation: Quantum computers represent quantum states using qubits, avoiding the exponential memory scaling that plagues classical computers. A system needing 2^N classical variables can be represented with N qubits, enabling simulation of larger molecules.

questions.question: For studying non-covalent interactions (like hydrogen bonding), which approach is recommended?
questions.explanation: Standard DFT poorly describes dispersion (London) forces, which are crucial for non-covalent interactions. Adding empirical dispersion corrections (like D3 or D4) or using specially designed functionals (like ωB97M-V) is essential.

questions.question: Which of the following are open-source computational chemistry packages? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: PySCF, Psi4, and OpenFermion are all open-source. Gaussian is commercial software. ORCA is free for academic use but not open-source.
